title: Rewrite B-Tree concept content: hook, intuition, WHY steps, complexity, real-world, summary
description: B-Tree concept scores 6/36 (Grade F). Complete rewrite needed. HOOK: 'Your database has 10 million users. Without an index, finding one user means checking ALL 10M rows. With a B-Tree index, 4 comparisons.' INTUITION: Library multi-level analogy. WHY STEPS: 'Key 35 is between 20 and 40, so we follow the MIDDLE pointer because B-Trees keep keys sorted.' COMPLEXITY: 'Watch the counter: 10 keys=2 steps, 1M keys=7 steps. Each 10x only adds 1 step.' REAL-WORLD: 'PostgreSQL creates a B+ Tree for every CREATE INDEX.' SUMMARY: 3-bullet flashcard.
acceptanceCriteria: Mode description replaced with problem-first hook mentioning 10M rows vs 4 comparisons
How It Works panel has intuition analogy BEFORE listing operations
Step descriptions include WHY reasoning (e.g., 'because 35 > 20 and 35 < 40')
Complexity section with concrete example: '1000 keys = 4 steps, 1M keys = 7'
Real-world section: PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite cited
3-bullet summary suitable for flashcard

title: Rewrite Normalization concept: hook, intuition, WHY steps, real-world, summary
description: Normalization scores 5/36 (Grade F). Worst teaching in module. HOOK: 'Your spreadsheet repeats customer address on every order row. Update one, miss another — now you have two addresses. Normalization prevents this.' INTUITION: Messy desk analogy — post-its with everything vs organized drawers. WHY: 'CourseID → Instructor → InstructorOffice is a TRANSITIVE dependency chain. To fix: separate Instructor data into its own table.' REAL-WORLD: Instagram user rename example. SUMMARY: 1NF=atomic, 2NF=no partial, 3NF=no transitive.
acceptanceCriteria: Mode description rewritten with redundancy/update anomaly problem framing
Intuition analogy added before technical content (messy desk or closet organizing)
FD notation (→) explained on first appearance: 'The arrow means DETERMINES'
Each decomposition step explains WHY in terms of dependency violations
Real-world example (Instagram/Uber scale) in properties panel
3-line summary: 1NF, 2NF, 3NF in plain English

title: Upgrade Transaction Isolation content: hook, complexity tradeoff, real-world, summary
description: Transaction Isolation scores 12/36 (Grade D) — best in module but still below target. Already has excellent anomaly explanations (T4=3) and visualization (T3=3). Needs: better hook (concert ticket double-sell), complexity tradeoff ('READ COMMITTED is 2-5x faster than SERIALIZABLE'), real-world ('PostgreSQL defaults to READ COMMITTED, MySQL to REPEATABLE READ'), and concise summary table (Level → Prevents → Allows).
acceptanceCriteria: Hook rewritten: concert ticket / bank transfer scenario instead of dry description
Performance tradeoff section: 'Higher isolation = fewer bugs but slower'
Real-world: which databases default to which level, and why
Summary table: 4 rows showing Level / Prevents / Allows at a glance
Existing anomaly explanations PRESERVED (they're already excellent)

title: Rewrite LSM-Tree concept: hook, desk analogy, amplification tradeoff, real-world, summary
description: LSM-Tree scores 9/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'B-Trees are great for reading but every write does random disk I/O. LSM-Trees flip this: writes are sequential and lightning fast. That's why Cassandra can handle 1 million writes per second.' INTUITION: 'Memtable = your desk (fast, small). SSTables = filing cabinet (sorted, slower). When desk is full, file everything away.' TRADEOFF: Write amplification vs read amplification vs space amplification (RUM conjecture). REAL-WORLD: RocksDB, Cassandra, LevelDB, TiKV.
acceptanceCriteria: Hook explains WHY LSM exists: sequential writes beat random writes
Desk/filing cabinet analogy in How It Works panel before operations list
Write/read/space amplification tradeoff explained with concrete numbers
Real-world: 'RocksDB (CockroachDB, TiKV), Cassandra, LevelDB' cited
Comparison with B-Tree: 'B-Tree = fast reads, LSM = fast writes'
3-bullet summary: write path, read path, compaction purpose

title: Rewrite Hash Index concept: hook, filing cabinet analogy, O(1) vs O(n), real-world, summary
description: Hash Index scores 8/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'Need to find one record by exact key? Hash indexes do it in ONE step — constant time regardless of table size. But they can't sort or range scan.' INTUITION: Filing cabinet with labeled drawers. Hash function = label system. COMPLEXITY: 'O(1) average — but if everything lands in one drawer (worst case), it's O(n). That's why good hash functions matter.' REAL-WORLD: PostgreSQL hash indexes, Memcached, Redis.
acceptanceCriteria: Hook contrasts hash O(1) equality lookup with B-Tree O(log n) range support
Filing cabinet analogy in How It Works panel
Complexity explains average vs worst case with drawer overflow visual
Real-world: PostgreSQL hash index, Memcached, Redis cited
Comparison callout: 'Use hash for equality (WHERE email=X), B-Tree for ranges (WHERE age > 25)'
3-bullet summary

title: Rewrite ER Diagram concept: hook, entity-relationship intuition, guided walkthrough, summary
description: ER Diagram scores 6/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'Before writing any SQL, you need a blueprint. ER diagrams are the architectural drawings of your database — get them wrong and you'll be fixing schema problems for years.' INTUITION: 'Entities are the THINGS you track (Users, Orders). Attributes are the FACTS about them (name, email). Relationships are the CONNECTIONS (a User places many Orders).' GUIDED: Walk user through building the Student-Course schema step by step. SUMMARY: Entity = table, Attribute = column, Relationship = foreign key.
acceptanceCriteria: Hook rewritten: blueprint analogy, consequences of bad design
Intuition text: 'Entities = things, Attributes = facts, Relationships = connections'
Guided first-time experience: tooltip prompts for 'Click an entity to edit' and 'Add a relationship'
Each sample schema has 1-sentence explanation of WHY it's designed that way
3-bullet summary mapping ER notation to SQL: entity=table, attribute=column, relationship=FK

title: Rewrite Query Plan concept: GPS analogy, actionable advice per node type, real-world EXPLAIN
description: Query Plans scores 7/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'You write SELECT * FROM users WHERE email=X. The database doesn't just execute it — it builds a PLAN, like Google Maps choosing the fastest route. A bad plan means your 1ms query takes 30 seconds.' INTUITION: 'The query optimizer is like a GPS: it considers multiple routes (SeqScan, IndexScan, HashJoin) and picks the cheapest.' ACTIONABLE: Each node type gets 'What to do': SeqScan → 'Add an index on the filtered column.' REAL-WORLD: 'This is what PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook: GPS/route planning analogy for query optimization
Each node type (SeqScan, IndexScan, HashJoin, Sort, Aggregate, Filter) has 'What to do' advice
SeqScan explicitly marked: 'This means NO index is used — usually a performance problem'
IndexScan marked: 'Good! An index is being used. Check if it's the right one.'
Example EXPLAIN output shown alongside our generated plan for comparison
Summary: 'Red = expensive. SeqScan = missing index. HashJoin = check join key.'

title: Rewrite Sharding concept: city planning analogy, hot partition demo, consistent hashing teaser
description: Sharding scores 9/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'Your database has 1 billion rows. One server can't hold them all. Sharding splits data across multiple servers — but split WRONG and one server melts while others sit idle.' INTUITION: City planning — hash=assign by name hash (even but no locality), range=assign by zip code (natural but hotspots), directory=city hall lookup (flexible but bottleneck). DEMO: Explicitly show a hot partition scenario. TEASER: 'There's a 4th strategy — Consistent Hashing — that minimizes data movement when servers join/leave.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook rewritten: billion rows, one server can't hold it, wrong split = hot partition
City planning analogy for each of 3 strategies
Hot partition explicitly demonstrated with visual warning: 'This shard has 5x average load'
Each strategy has 'When to use' advice: hash=even distribution, range=range queries, directory=flexible routing
Consistent hashing teaser linking to planned DBL-073 content
Summary: 'Hash = even, Range = locality, Directory = flexible, Consistent = minimal rebalancing'

title: Rewrite Consistency Levels concept: ATM analogy, partition demo, CAP connection
description: Consistency Levels scores 12/36 (Grade D). HOOK: 'You check your bank balance on your phone (replica 1) and see $1000. Your partner checks on their phone (replica 2) and sees $950. Who's right? It depends on the consistency level.' INTUITION: ATM analogy — strong=ATM calls bank HQ before showing balance (slow, accurate), eventual=ATM shows cached balance (fast, maybe stale). ADD: Connection to CAP theorem ('Strong consistency + partition tolerance = reduced availability').
acceptanceCriteria: Hook: bank balance / ATM scenario showing stale read impact
ATM analogy for each level (strong=call HQ, eventual=cached, causal=ordered updates)
CAP theorem connection: 'In a network partition, you must choose between consistency and availability'
Latency numbers made concrete: 'Strong: 50-200ms. Eventual: 5-20ms. That's 10x difference.'
Summary: 'Strong = always correct but slow. Eventual = fast but may be stale. Causal = ordered but not instant.'

title: Rewrite Replication Lag concept: social media feed analogy, partition healing, production impact
description: Replication Lag scores 7/36 (Grade F). HOOK: 'You post a photo on Instagram. Your friend refreshes — it's not there. They refresh again 2 seconds later — now it appears. That 2-second gap IS replication lag.' INTUITION: Library branch analogy — main library gets new book, branches get copies later. DEMO: Show read-your-writes inconsistency: write to primary, immediately read from replica, get stale data. REAL-WORLD: 'Every database with replicas has lag. PostgreSQL streaming replication typically has <1ms lag, but under load it can spike to seconds.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook: Instagram photo / social media feed lag scenario
Library branch analogy for primary→replica relationship
Read-your-writes inconsistency demonstrated: 'You just wrote v5, but replica still shows v3'
Partition healing animation: what happens when a partitioned replica reconnects
Real-world lag numbers: PostgreSQL <1ms typical, seconds under load
Summary: 'Lag = time for replicas to catch up. More replicas = more availability but more lag risk.'

title: Rewrite Query Plan Simulation concept: before/after index revelation, cost intuition, interview framing
description: Query Plan Simulation scores 10/36 (Grade F). The INDEX TOGGLING feature is excellent (T7=2) but underexplained. HOOK: 'This query scans 5 million rows. Add ONE index and it reads 5 rows. Same result, 1,000,000x less work. Watch.' REVELATION: First show the plan WITHOUT index (SeqScan, red, cost=67500). Then user toggles index ON. Plan TRANSFORMS to IndexScan (green, cost=12). MAKE THIS THE AHA MOMENT. INTERVIEW: 'In interviews, they'll show you a slow query and ask how to fix it. The answer is usually: add an index and check the plan.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook text frames the index toggle as a dramatic reveal: 'Watch what one index does'
Default state shows a SeqScan plan with prominent 'SLOW' indicator
Toggling index shows animated cost reduction with delta badge (-99.8%)
Interview tip: 'When asked to optimize a query, start with EXPLAIN and check for SeqScan'
Each of 5 sample queries has a 1-sentence 'Why this matters' annotation
